claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children are you enquiring about, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'm enquiring about my child Alex, who is 19 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 19 years old, they fall into the 16-19 age category where eligibility depends on their current situation.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** 

(Approved education typically includes A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications – but not higher education such as a university degree or apprenticeships.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in education - he's studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that Alex is studying for his A-levels – that counts as approved education.

Now I have a couple more questions to check your eligibility:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to understand your responsibility for Alex:

**Does Alex live with you, or if not, do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit (currently £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child) towards looking after him – for example on food, clothes, or pocket money?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Does Alex work 24 hours or more per week in paid employment?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does work - he has a part-time job at the supermarket alongside his studies, and it is more than 24 hours a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information.

Unfortunately, this affects Alex's eligibility. According to the Child Benefit rules, you will stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for **24 hours or more a week**, even if they are still in approved education or training.

I have one final question to confirm:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or receiving certain benefits (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit) in Alex's name?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually someone else is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex - his other parent who also lives with him claims it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is very helpful information.

Based on everything you've told me, I now have enough information to make an eligibility assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**. There are two reasons for this:

1. **Working hours**: Although Alex is 19 and studying for his A-levels (which is approved education), he works **24 or more hours per week** in paid employment. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child works 24 hours or more per week, even if they're still in approved education or training.

2. **Another person is already claiming**: Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child. Since Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him, you cannot also claim for Alex.

If Alex's working hours were to reduce to below 24 hours per week in the future, and the other parent stopped their claim, you may be able to make a new claim at that point.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and studying for A-levels, which is approved education. However, there are two key issues that affect eligibility: 1) Alex works more than 24 hours per week in paid employment. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week, even if they remain in approved education or training. This alone would make the claimant ineligible. 2) Additionally, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Therefore, the claimant cannot also receive Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex due to two reasons: (1) Alex works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment, which disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit regardless of Alex's education status; and (2) another person (Alex's other parent) is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child.

However, there are two key issues that affect eligibility: 1) Alex works more than 24 hours per week in paid employment. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week, even if they remain in approved education or training. This alone would make the claimant ineligible. 2) Additionally, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Therefore, the claimant cannot also receive Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex due to two reasons: (1) Alex works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment, which disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit regardless of Alex's education status; and (2) another person (Alex's other parent) is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child.
